key_points: Hardesty Hall recommended most for social freshmen
Honors dorms have no real difference from regular dorms
Limited student discussion suggests housing isn't a major complaint
key_quotes: Hardesty or John Mabee
There's really no difference, you could go to the first or second floor anytime you wanted

key_points: Small campus means limited social scene outside organizations
Party scene exists but not prominently discussed
D1 athletics provide some campus energy and community
key_quotes: Go to the buck
I've always liked Tulsa for some reason
There's enough diversity in interests/organizations that you stand a pretty good chance of making a good friend group

key_points: No student comments found about dining halls
Cannot assess quality or variety from available data
Absence of data is notable — not a selling point
No direct student comments about mental health resources
Competitive private university atmosphere noted as stressful
Small campus means less anonymity which can add pressure
key_quotes: because it's a competitive private university, there is occasionally a sense of stress

key_points: National Merit scholarship recently cut — no longer a full ride
Now covers only tuition and housing, not fees or food
Strong merit aid still available but less generous than advertised
key_quotes: the University of Tulsa's national merit semifinalist scholarship package is no longer a full-ride. It only covers tuition and housing
Not even my safety school is completely affordable anymore. I'm so cooked

So one thing about TU is they absolutely provide a lot of financial aid. Speak to your enrollment advisor and usually if you tell them the number you need in order to commit they'll find a way to get there

key_points: STEM and chemistry programs genuinely strong with research access
Liberal arts cuts in 2019 gutted multiple departments
Small class sizes enable undergrad research opportunities rare elsewhere
key_quotes: She already has been hooked up with working on research with a professor (something that not a lot of schools can offer a freshman)
TU is a great school but only for specific areas of study. If you are going for English or general Humanities I would steer clear
The chemistry program is strong at TU, and TURC is a great opportunity if you are planning on going to grad school

key_points: School described as 'broke as hell' by recent 2025 grad
2019 liberal arts restructuring cut programs with no graduates in 5 years
George Kaiser's corporate influence over university direction is a documented concern
key_quotes: as a new grad (2025), don't attend TU unless you absolutely have to — the school is broke as hell
The president sent an update with more info not long ago which had more insight into why cuts were made
TU doesn't have clinical sciences as a historic strength... TU has been having some financial difficulties lately

key_points: Campus widely praised as beautiful and easy to navigate
Tulsa city itself offers real cultural amenities and food scene
Location in Tulsa is genuinely good — not a college town wasteland
key_quotes: Beautiful (and easily navigable) campus, fantastic people, incredible city culture
The campus is awesome, and it's a good location in the city

key_points: A Fortune 50 CEO (Walmart) attended TU for undergrad
STEM and engineering grads have solid outcomes especially in oil/gas
Prestige is low nationally but regional Oklahoma/energy sector network is real
key_quotes: Walmart - University of Tulsa
I'm a premed (bio/psych major) at TU who was recently admitted to medical school. Several of my classmates have also earned an acceptance to schools across the country
TU is a great uni... for specific areas of study

key_points: With scholarships many students pay under $5k/year — exceptional value
National Merit package reduction makes cost calculus less favorable now
Full sticker price not worth it — scholarships are essential
key_quotes: With the scholarship they offered from high school GPA and other scholarships I've applied towards, I only pay around 5k a year
They have a pretty campus that's about it, the cost does not reflect the academic experience
The cheapest reputable school is always the right answer

key_points: Best fit for STEM students who land strong merit aid
Small, tight-knit campus with real research access for undergrads
Liberal arts and humanities students should look elsewhere
key_quotes: Find somewhere else, if I had the opportunity to go back in time and choose where I went to college I would
Loved my TU experience! Obviously anywhere you go, there's a wide range of people
It's been worth it for me

Enrollment advisors will negotiate aid packages if you push back
Small cohort means professors actually know your name and invest in you
